course: GA Zero to Hero: Absolute Beginner
title: Your First Chord — E Minor
objectives: Understand what a chord is — multiple notes sounding together
Play the Em (E minor) chord cleanly on guitar
Transition between open strings and Em chord

sections.title: What Is a Chord?
sections.body:
A chord is simply two or more notes played at the same time. When you strum all six strings of your guitar open (without pressing any frets), you're already playing a chord! It might not sound like much yet, but that's where we start.

Chords are the building blocks of songs. Most pop, rock, and folk songs use just 3-5 chords. By the end of this course, you'll know enough chords to play hundreds of songs.
sections.practice_exercise: Strum all six open strings slowly, one at a time from low (thick) to high (thin). Then strum them all together. Listen to the sound — that's the open strings ringing together.

sections.title: How to Read a Chord Diagram
sections.body:
A chord diagram is a picture of your guitar neck:

- **Vertical lines** = strings (low E on the left, high E on the right)
- **Horizontal lines** = frets
- **Black dots** = where to place your fingers
- **Numbers** = which finger to use (1=index, 2=middle, 3=ring, 4=pinky)
- **O** above a string = play it open (don't press anything)
- **X** above a string = don't play this string

For Em, all strings are played open except two — your fingers go on the 2nd fret of the A string and the 2nd fret of the D string.

sections.title: Playing E Minor (Em)
sections.body:
Em is the easiest chord on guitar. Here's why: you only need two fingers, and all six strings ring out.

**Finger placement:**
1. Place your **middle finger (2)** on the **2nd fret** of the **A string** (5th string)
2. Place your **ring finger (3)** on the **2nd fret** of the **D string** (4th string)
3. All other strings are open — don't press anything else

**Tips:**
- Press down firmly, right behind the fret (not on top of it)
- Curve your fingers so you don't accidentally mute the neighboring strings
- Your thumb should rest behind the neck, roughly opposite your middle finger
sections.practice_exercise: Place your fingers for Em. Strum slowly from the low E string (thickest) to the high E string (thinnest). Every string should ring clearly. If any string sounds buzzy or dead, adjust your finger pressure and position. Repeat 10 times.

sections.title: Your First Strum Pattern
sections.body:
Now that you can hold the Em chord, let's make music with it.

**Basic down-strum pattern:**
- Strum DOWN across all strings, evenly and smoothly
- Count: 1 - 2 - 3 - 4 (one strum per beat)
- Keep a steady rhythm — use your foot to tap the beat
- Strum from your wrist, not your whole arm

**Once that's comfortable, try this pattern:**
- DOWN - DOWN - DOWN - DOWN (4 beats)
- Lift fingers off → strum open strings (4 beats)
- Place fingers back → Em again (4 beats)

Congratulations — you're playing a chord progression!
sections.practice_exercise: Set a timer for 2 minutes. Strum Em with steady down-strums, counting 1-2-3-4 out loud. Every 4 bars (16 beats), lift your fingers and strum open strings for 4 bars, then go back to Em. This builds your muscle memory for chord changes.

sections.title: Why Em? The Alchemy Begins
sections.body:
We started with Em for a reason. In the alchemical tradition, the Nigredo (blackening) stage is about breaking down — letting go of what you think you know and starting from raw material.

Em is the rawest material on guitar: two fingers, six strings, a deep melancholic sound that has powered thousands of songs from Nirvana to Bob Marley. It's dark, it's simple, it's powerful.

Next module, we'll add Am (A minor) — and with just two chords, you'll be able to play your first real song.

key_terms.definition: Two or more notes played simultaneously
Sweeping across multiple strings with a pick or fingers
The metal strips on the guitar neck that change the pitch when you press behind them
A string played without pressing any fret

questions.question: How many fingers do you need to play Em?
questions.answer: Two — middle finger on A string 2nd fret, ring finger on D string 2nd fret
questions.hint: It's the easiest chord because it uses the fewest fingers

questions.question: When you strum Em, how many strings should ring out?
questions.hint: Em is special — no strings are muted (no X marks in the diagram)

questions.question: If a string sounds buzzy, what should you check?
questions.answer: Finger pressure (press harder) and position (press just behind the fret, not on top of it). Also check that your fingers aren't touching neighboring strings.
questions.hint: The two most common problems for beginners

assessment.pass_criteria: All six strings of the Em chord ring clearly without buzzing, and you can maintain a steady 4-beat strum pattern for 30 seconds

findings: Em requires the fewest finger placements of any standard chord — optimal for first lesson
Muscle memory for chord shapes develops fastest with 2-minute focused repetition blocks
Beginning with minor chords creates emotional engagement faster than major chords
sources: Guitar pedagogy consensus — Em as first chord is nearly universal
Savoir sans Frontières curriculum reference — learning by doing
